items.front: What principle governs route use here?
items.back: Clear aircraft via routes consistent with the altitude stratum in which the operation will be conducted.

items.front: When a GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on a random RNAV route is not being radar monitored, what kind of route must it be on?
What kinds of points may define that point-to-point route?
items.back: Published NAVAIDs, waypoints, fixes, or airports recallable from the aircraft navigation database and displayed on controller maps or charts.

items.front: When applying nonradar separation on a random RNAV route, what is the maximum distance between points?
How much protected airspace is required either side of the route centerline on that random RNAV segment?
What altitude condition applies to assigned altitudes on that projected route segment?
items.back: They must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment, including its protected airspace.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a random impromptu route?
What phraseology is used for a point-to-point segment beginning after a fix?
What phraseology may be used to clear an aircraft within a quadrant of a NAVAID?
items.back: Cleared to fly (specified) quadrant of (NAVAID name and type) within (number of miles) mile radius.

items.front: What additional service is required when RNAV aircraft transition between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes?
What is one approved route-structure transition method here?
May route-structure transitions be made by vectoring aircraft to or from radials, courses, or azimuths of the ATS route assigned?
May departing or arriving aircraft be cleared directly to or between the NAVAIDs forming the ATS route assigned?
For the random RNAV transition method, what kinds of points may be used on the new route?
items.back: A NAVAID, waypoint, airport, or fix on the new route.

items.front: Below FL180, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes on a military route segment?
At FL180 and above, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes?
What three elements compose a degree-distance fix?
items.back: A location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, and distance in miles from the NAVAID used.

items.front: How are degree-distance fixes used to define a route treated for reporting purposes unless ATC authorizes otherwise?
What is the sample degree-distance fix given in the paragraph?
What NAVAID-use restriction applies to degree-distance fixes?
items.back: The selected NAVAIDs must be authorized for use at the altitude being flown and within their published service volume area.

items.front: When a depicted alternative route is issued to a non-RNAV aircraft because part of a route is unusable, what word must immediately precede that route?
What is one approved non-RNAV alternative route type when part of an airway or route is unusable because of NAVAID status?
items.back: A route defined by NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.

items.front: What is one other approved non-RNAV alternative route type here?
items.back: A route defined as direct to or between NAVAIDs.

items.front: What is one more approved non-RNAV alternative route type here?
What must RNAV aircraft be told if they will proceed to the location of the unusable NAVAID?
items.back: Inform them of the NAVAID outage.

items.front: When may routes through Class G airspace be included in a clearance?
items.back: Only when requested by the pilot.

items.front: What filed route is considered a pilot request for routing through Class G airspace?
items.back: A random RNAV route filed through Class G airspace.

items.front: What filed route is also considered a pilot request for routing through Class G airspace?
items.back: A flight plan containing MTR segments in or through Class G airspace.

items.front: Are separation criteria applicable in Class G airspace?
What ATC services are still applicable in Class G airspace to aircraft in direct communication with ATC?
En route, when may you issue a revised routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in your facility's airspace?
items.back: Only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.

items.front: What two conditions trigger the basic restriction against taking an aircraft off its route unless operational necessity dictates?
items.back: The aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative, or the route under consideration is within a protected segment.

items.front: If a protected-segment routing amendment is made by an ATCS, with whom must coordination occur?
If a protected-segment routing amendment is made by a terminal facility TMU, with whom must coordination occur?
If a protected-segment routing amendment is outside the ARTCC TMU's own facility, with whom must coordination occur?
What does the note say this paragraph must not preclude?
items.back: Routing clearances that conform to an LOA or SOP, or are required to maintain separation or comply with traffic flow management initiatives.
